text: The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and makes an incision inferior to it, adjusting the dissection plane towards the terminal ileum. The assistant holds the transverse colon for better visualization of the ileocolic vessels, facilitating posterior dissection. The duodenum is identified during a medial to lateral dissection, with the ureter also visible. The ileocolic pedicle is cleaned off, preparing for ligation using a vessel sealer extend.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, clearing tissue off the duodenum and dropping the transverse colon to mobilize the hepatic flexure. Adhesions to the gallbladder are freed, allowing retraction of the gallbladder and liver cephalad to expose the hepatic flexure. The transverse colon is separated from the gallbladder, revealing a clear dissection plane. The surgeon uses scissors to free lateral attachments, with the appendix and terminal ileum coming into view as the right colon is mobilized.

The surgeon cleans off the proximal and distal mesentery, focusing on the terminal ileum. The right branch of the middle colic artery is identified and cauterized twice before being taken. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to check perfusion. The specimen is placed aside, and attention is turned towards the anastomosis.

The surgeon prepares for a side-to-side isoparastaltic anastomosis using a 60mm stapler with a white load for the anastomosis and a blue load for bowel transection. The stapler is inserted through the suprapubic port, which will be extended for the extraction site. The surgeon uses a 3 OV lock suture to close the common channel, initially making a mistake by stitching against the grain, causing tissue oozing, which is corrected with a canal stitch.
